key_insight: An interpretable urinary metabolite machine-learning score (uLCI) can non-invasively detect lung cancer and track increasing risk across disease stages.
clinical_relevance: If validated in broader real-world settings, uLCI could complement imaging by helping identify lung cancer earlier and potentially triage patients more efficiently.
method: Combined 4 urinary metabolites with age, race, and smoking status.
Built the score using Lasso-regularized logistic regression.
Developed in one cohort and tested without refitting in an independent validation cohort.
evidence_signal: The model achieved AUC 0.906 in the development cohort and 0.748 in the independent cohort, with monotonic stage-wise increases and added value over clinical variables.
watch_item: Performance dropped in the external cohort, so further validation is needed before routine implementation across diverse populations and clinical settings.
paper_title: The urinary-metabolite-based lung cancer index (uLCI): an interpretable machine-learning risk model for early-stage disease
